01-01.text: The original American Telephone & Telegraph Company is divested of its 22 Bell System companies as a result of the settlement of the 1974 United States Department of Justice antitrust suit against AT&T.

01-02.text: Georgia becomes the fourth state to ratify the United States Constitution.

01-03.text: Alaska was admitted as the 49th state, changing representation in Congress and the Electoral College.
Frances P. Bolton and her son, Oliver from Ohio, become the first mother and son to serve simultaneously in the U.S. Congress.

01-04.text: United States President Richard Nixon refuses to hand over materials subpoenaed by the Senate Watergate Committee.

01-05.text: In a speech given to the United States Congress, United States President Dwight D. Eisenhower announces the establishment of what will later be called the Eisenhower Doctrine.

01-06.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt delivered the Four Freedoms speech to Congress.
01-06.ap_connection: Presidential agenda setting; civil liberties; foreign policy

01-06.text: Congress reconvened after the January 6 attack and certified the Electoral College results.
01-06.ap_connection: Electoral College; peaceful transfer of power; Congress

01-06.text: American Civil Rights Movement: Edgar Ray Killen is arrested as a suspect in the 1964 murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner.
01-06.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

01-07.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

01-08.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

01-09.text: Connecticut becomes the fifth state to ratify the Constitution.

01-10.text: Holy See-United States relations: The United States and Holy See (Vatican City) re-establish full diplomatic relations after almost 117 years, overturning the United States Congress's 1867 ban on public funding for such a diplomatic envoy.

01-11.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

01-12.text: Persian Gulf War: An act of the U.S. Congress authorizes the use of American military force to drive Iraq out of Kuwait.
Hattie Caraway becomes the first woman elected to the United States Senate.

01-13.text: Civic focus: Public Opinion — The public’s political attitudes influence elections and policymaking.

01-14.text: American Revolutionary War: Ratification Day, United States - Congress ratifies the Treaty of Paris with Great Britain.
The "Fundamental Orders", the first written constitution that created a government, is adopted in Connecticut.

01-15.text: Martin Luther King Jr. was born; his leadership later helped push federal civil rights action.
01-15.ap_connection: Civil rights movement; social movements; federal legislation

01-16.text: Temperance movement: The United States ratifies the Eighteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, requiring Prohibition in the United States one year after ratification.
The United States Senate accepts the Anglo-German treaty of 1899 in which the United Kingdom renounces its claims to the Samoan islands.

01-17.text: Civic focus: Campaign Finance — Rules governing political money shape electoral competition and political speech.

01-18.text: Civic focus: Agenda Setting — Political institutions and media influence which issues receive public attention.

01-19.text: Japan and the United States sign the US-Japan Mutual Security Treaty
01-19.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-19.text: The United States Senate votes against joining the League of Nations.

01-20.text: The first presidential inauguration under the 20th Amendment was held, moving Inauguration Day from March to January.

01-21.text: American Civil War: Jefferson Davis resigns from the United States Senate.

01-22.text: The Supreme Court decided Roe v. Wade, recognizing a constitutional right to abortion under privacy doctrine.
01-22.ap_connection: Civil liberties; privacy; substantive due process

01-22.text: The Supreme Court of the United States delivers its decisions in Roe v. Wade and Doe v. Bolton, legalizing elective abortion in all fifty states.
01-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

01-23.text: The 24th Amendment to the United States Constitution, prohibiting the use of poll taxes in national elections, is ratified.
Charles Lindbergh testifies before the U.S. Congress and recommends that the United States negotiate a neutrality pact with Adolf Hitler.

01-24.text: The 24th Amendment was ratified, banning poll taxes in federal elections.
The 20th Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, changing the beginning and end of terms for all elected federal offices.
In Brushaber v. Union Pacific Railroad Co., the Supreme Court of the United States declares the federal income tax constitutional.
01-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

01-25.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

01-26.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

01-27.text: The U.S. Congress approves Indian Territory (in what is present-day Oklahoma), clearing the way for forced relocation of the Eastern Indians on the "Trail of Tears".

United States, United Kingdom, and Soviet Union sign the Outer Space Treaty in Washington, D.C., banning deployment of nuclear weapons in space, and limiting use of the Moon and other celestial bodies to peaceful purposes.
01-27.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-28.text: An act of the U.S. Congress creates the United States Coast Guard as a branch of the United States Armed Forces.

01-29.text: Governor of Illinois Rod Blagojevich is removed from office following his conviction of several corruption charges, including the alleged solicitation of personal benefit in exchange for an appointment to the United States Senate as a replacement for then-U.S. president-elect Barack Obama.

01-30.text: African-American civil rights leader Martin Luther King, Jr.'s home is bombed in retaliation for the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
01-30.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

01-31.text: American Civil War: The United States Congress passes the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, abolishing slavery and submits it to the states for ratification.

02-01.text: Four students began the Greensboro sit-ins, a major civil rights protest against segregation.
02-01.ap_connection: Civil rights; linkage institutions; social movements

02-01.text: President Abraham Lincoln signs the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution.

02-02.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

02-03.text: The 15th Amendment was ratified, prohibiting voting discrimination based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
02-03.ap_connection: Voting rights; Reconstruction amendments; political participation

02-03.text: The 16th Amendment was ratified, authorizing Congress to collect a federal income tax.
The Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, guaranteeing voting rights to male citizens regardless of race.

02-04.text: George Washington is unanimously elected as the first President of the United States by the U.S. Electoral College.

02-05.text: The Congress of the United States passes the Immigration Act of 1917 over President Woodrow Wilson's veto.
South Carolina becomes the second state to ratify the Articles of Confederation.

02-06.text: Massachusetts becomes the sixth state to ratify the United States Constitution.

In testimony before a United States Senate subcommittee, Lockheed Corporation president Carl Kotchian admits that the company had paid out approximately $3 million in bribes to the office of Japanese Prime Minister Kakuei Tanaka.
Spanish-American War: The Treaty of Paris, a peace treaty between the United States and Spain, is ratified by the United States Senate.

02-07.text: The U.S. state of Mississippi officially certifies the Thirteenth Amendment, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment was formally ratified by Mississippi in 1995.
The 11th Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified.

02-08.text: Delaware refuses to ratify the Thirteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. Slavery was outlawed in the United States, including Delaware, when the Amendment was ratified by the requisite number of states on December 6, 1865. Delaware ratified the Thirteenth Amendment on February 12, 1901, which was the ninety-second anniversary of the birth of Abraham Lincoln.

02-09.text: After no candidate receives a majority of electoral votes in the US presidential election of 1824, the United States House of Representatives elects John Quincy Adams as President of the United States.
US president Grover Cleveland signs a bill elevating the United States Department of Agriculture to a Cabinet-level agency.
American Civil War: Jefferson Davis is elected the Provisional President of the Confederate States of America by the Confederate convention at Montgomery, Alabama.

02-10.text: The 25th Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified.

02-11.text: Eighty-seven countries, including the United States, United Kingdom, and Soviet Union, sign the Seabed Arms Control Treaty outlawing nuclear weapons on the ocean floor in international waters.
02-11.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

02-11.text: American Civil War: The United States House of Representatives unanimously passes a resolution guaranteeing noninterference with slavery in any state.

02-12.text: Abraham Lincoln was born; his presidency tested executive power, federalism, and constitutional union.
United States President Bill Clinton is acquitted by the United States Senate in his impeachment trial.

African American United States Army veteran Isaac Woodard is severely beaten by a South Carolina police officer to the point where he loses his vision in both eyes. The incident later galvanizes the Civil Rights Movement and partially inspires Orson Welles' film Touch of Evil.
02-12.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

02-13.text: Civic focus: Public Opinion — The public’s political attitudes influence elections and policymaking.

02-14.text: Civic focus: Linkage Institutions — Parties, elections, interest groups, and media connect people to government.

02-15.text: Women's rights: US President Rutherford B. Hayes signs a bill allowing female attorneys to argue cases before the Supreme Court of the United States.
02-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-16.text: Civic focus: Interest Groups — Organized groups seek to influence public policy without directly controlling government.

02-17.text: In Wesberry v. Sanders the Supreme Court of the United States rules that congressional districts have to be approximately equal in population.
02-17.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-17.text: An electoral tie between Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr is resolved when Jefferson is elected President of the United States and Burr, Vice President by the United States House of Representatives.

02-18.text: Congress passes a law admitting the state of Vermont to the Union, effective 4 March, after that state had existed for 14 years as a de facto independent largely unrecognized state.

02-19.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 9066, authorizing Japanese American internment during World War II.

02-20.text: The Congress of the United States proposes the Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution that will end Prohibition in the United States.
The Congress of the United States approves the construction of the San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge by the state of California.

02-21.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

02-22.text: Aldrich Ames and his wife are charged by the United States Department of Justice with spying for the Soviet Union.
By the Adams-Onís Treaty, Spain sells Florida to the United States for five million U.S. dollars.
02-22.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

02-23.text: U.S. President Calvin Coolidge signs a bill by Congress establishing the Federal Radio Commission (later replaced by the Federal Communications Commission) which was to regulate the use of radio frequencies in the United States.

02-24.text: The Supreme Court decided Marbury v. Madison, establishing judicial review.
02-24.ap_connection: Judicial review; Supreme Court; checks and balances

02-24.text: Andrew Johnson becomes the first President of the United States to be impeached by the United States House of Representatives. He is later acquitted in the Senate.
A special commission of the United States Congress condemns the Japanese American internment during World War II.

02-25.text: Hiram Rhodes Revels, a Republican from Mississippi, is sworn into the United States Senate, becoming the first African American ever to sit in the U.S. Congress.

02-26.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

02-27.text: The 22nd Amendment was ratified, limiting presidents to two elected terms.
02-27.ap_connection: Presidency; constitutional amendments; checks on power

02-27.text: United States labor law: The U.S. Supreme Court rules that sit-down strikes violate property owners' rights and are therefore illegal.
02-27.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-27.text: A challenge to the Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, allowing women the right to vote, is rebuffed by the Supreme Court of the United States in Leser v. Garnett.
02-27.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-28.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

02-29.text: The Jay Treaty between the United States and Great Britain comes into force, facilitating ten years of peaceful trade between the two nations.
02-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-01.text: The Articles of Confederation went into effect, creating the first national government of the United States.
03-01.ap_connection: Articles of Confederation; federalism; weaknesses of national government

03-02.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

03-03.text: Censorship in the United States: The U.S. Congress enacts the Comstock Law, making it illegal to send any "obscene, lewd, or lascivious" books through the mail.
The U.S. Congress passes the Missouri Compromise.

03-04.text: The Constitution took effect as the framework for the new federal government.

Gay rights: Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services, Inc.: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that federal laws banning on-the-job sexual harassment also apply when both parties are the same sex.
03-04.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

03-05.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

03-06.text: The Supreme Court decided Dred Scott v. Sandford, denying citizenship to enslaved and formerly enslaved Black people.
03-06.ap_connection: Civil rights; Supreme Court; constitutional conflict

03-07.text: Bloody Sunday: a group of 600 civil rights marchers is brutally attacked by state and local police in Selma, Alabama.
03-07.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

03-08.text: The 1957 Georgia Memorial to Congress, which petitions the U.S. Congress to declare the ratification of the 14th and 15th Amendments to the U.S. Constitution null and void, is adopted by the U.S. state of Georgia.
The United States Senate votes to limit filibusters by adopting the cloture rule.

03-09.text: The U.S. Supreme Court rules in the United States v. The Amistad case that captive Africans who had seized control of the ship carrying them had been taken into slavery illegally.
03-09.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

03-10.text: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo is ratified by the United States Senate, ending the Mexican-American War.

03-11.text: American Civil War: The Constitution of the Confederate States of America is adopted.
Janet Reno is confirmed by the United States Senate and sworn in the next day, becoming the first female Attorney General of the United States.

03-12.text: Civic focus: Political Socialization — Families, schools, peers, and media shape political beliefs.

03-13.text: The United States Department of Justice announces that Exxon has agreed to pay $1 billion for the clean-up of the Exxon Valdez oil spill in Alaska.

03-14.text: The Hay-Herrán Treaty, granting the United States the right to build the Panama Canal, is ratified by the United States Senate. The Colombian Senate would later reject the treaty.
The Gold Standard Act is ratified, placing United States currency on the gold standard.

03-15.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson addressed Congress after Selma and called for voting rights legislation.

03-16.text: Mississippi formally ratifies the Thirteenth Amendment, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment was officially ratified in 1865.

03-17.text: Civic focus: Campaign Finance — Rules governing political money shape electoral competition and political speech.

03-18.text: The Supreme Court decided Gideon v. Wainwright, requiring states to provide attorneys for defendants who cannot afford one in felony cases.
03-18.ap_connection: Due process; selective incorporation; rights of the accused

03-18.text: American Civil War: The Congress of the Confederate States adjourns for the last time.

03-19.text: The United States Senate rejects the Treaty of Versailles for the second time (the first time was on November 19, 1919).

03-20.text: The United States Senate ratifies a peace treaty with Japan.

03-21.text: US President Jimmy Carter announces a United States boycott of the 1980 Summer Olympics in Moscow to protest the Soviet war in Afghanistan.
Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. leads 3,200 people on the start of the third and finally successful civil rights march from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama.
03-21.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

03-22.text: Congress sent the Equal Rights Amendment to the states for ratification, though it did not become part of the Constitution.
In Eisenstadt v. Baird, the United States Supreme Court decides that unmarried persons have the right to possess contraceptives.
03-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

03-22.text: In North Carolina, William Woods Holden becomes the first governor of a U.S. state to be removed from office by impeachment.
03-22.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-23.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

03-24.text: United States Congress passes the Tydings-McDuffie Act, allowing the Philippines to become a self-governing commonwealth.

03-25.text: Civil rights activists led by Martin Luther King Jr. successfully complete their 4-day 50-mile march from Selma to the capitol in Montgomery, Alabama.
03-25.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

03-26.text: The Supreme Court decided Baker v. Carr, opening the door to federal court review of legislative districting.

03-27.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

03-28.text: Cold War: The United States Department of State releases the Acheson-Lilienthal Report, outlining a plan for the international control of nuclear power.
03-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-29.text: The Twenty-third Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, allowing residents of Washington, D.C., to vote in presidential elections.

03-30.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

03-31.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

04-01.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

04-02.text: President Woodrow Wilson asked Congress for a declaration of war against Germany.
The United States Congress passes the Foraker Act, giving Puerto Rico limited self-rule.

04-03.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

04-04.text: Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated, intensifying national debate over civil rights, protest, and federal action.
04-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; social movements; public policy

04-05.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

04-06.text: World War I: The United States declares war on Germany (see President Woodrow Wilson's address to Congress).
Huey P. Long, Governor of Louisiana, is impeached by the Louisiana House of Representatives.

04-07.text: Prohibition in the United States is repealed for beer of no more than 3.2% alcohol by weight, eight months before the ratification of the XXI amendment. (Now celebrated as National Beer Day in the United States of America)
A bulldozer kills Rev. Bruce W. Klunder, a civil rights activist, during a school segregation protest in Cleveland, Ohio, sparking a riot.
04-07.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

04-08.text: The 17th Amendment to the United States Constitution, requiring direct election of Senators, becomes law.
In Pollock v. Farmers' Loan & Trust Co. the Supreme Court of the United States declares unapportioned income tax to be unconstitutional.
04-08.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

04-09.text: Congress passed the Civil Rights Act of 1866 over President Andrew Johnson's veto.

The Journey of Reconciliation, the first interracial Freedom Ride begins through the upper South in violation of Jim Crow laws. The riders wanted enforcement of the United States Supreme Court's 1946 Irene Morgan decision that banned racial segregation in interstate travel.
04-09.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

04-09.text: Marian Anderson sings at the Lincoln Memorial, after being denied the right to sing at the Daughters of the American Revolution's Constitution Hall.

04-10.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

04-11.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

04-12.text: American Revolution: With the Halifax Resolves, the North Carolina Provincial Congress authorizes its Congressional delegation to vote for independence from Britain.

04-13.text: Thomas Jefferson was born; his ideas shaped the Declaration of Independence and debates over limited government.
04-13.ap_connection: Declaration of Independence; natural rights; limited government

04-13.text: The United States Treasury Department reintroduces the two-dollar bill as a Federal Reserve Note on Thomas Jefferson's 233rd birthday as part of the United States Bicentennial celebration.

04-14.text: Civic focus: Linkage Institutions — Parties, elections, interest groups, and media connect people to government.

04-15.text: Preliminary articles of peace ending the American Revolutionary War (or American War of Independence) are ratified.

At Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, Ella Baker leads a conference that results in the creation of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, one of the principal organizations of the Civil Rights Movement in the 1960s.
04-15.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

04-16.text: The United States Senate ratifies the Rush-Bagot Treaty, establishing the border with Canada.

04-17.text: The Supreme Court decided Lochner v. New York, limiting some economic regulations under liberty-of-contract reasoning.
04-17.ap_connection: Judicial review; economic liberty; due process

04-18.text: Civic focus: Agenda Setting — Political institutions and media influence which issues receive public attention.

04-19.text: Civic focus: Political Accountability — Elections, oversight, courts, and public scrutiny allow officials to be held responsible.

04-20.text: President William McKinley signed a joint resolution to Congress for declaration of War against Spain, beginning the Spanish-American War.
U.S. Congress passes an act creating the Wisconsin Territory.

04-21.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

04-22.text: The U.S. Congress passes the Coinage Act of 1864 that mandates that the inscription In God We Trust be placed on all coins minted as United States currency.
The United Kingdom, Japan and the United States sign the London Naval Treaty regulating submarine warfare and limiting shipbuilding.
04-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-23.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

04-24.text: The United States Library of Congress is established when President John Adams signs legislation to appropriate $5,000 to purchase "such books as may be necessary for the use of Congress".

04-25.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

04-26.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

04-27.text: Following the resignation and death of William P. Frye, a compromise is reached to rotate the office of President pro tempore of the United States Senate.

04-28.text: Maryland becomes the seventh state to ratify the United States Constitution.
Occupied Japan by the Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers: The United States occupation of Japan ends as the Treaty of San Francisco, ratified September 8, 1951, comes into force.

04-29.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

04-30.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

05-01.text: The Memphis Race Riots begin. In three days time, 46 blacks and two whites were killed. Reports of the atrocities influenced passage of the Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution.

05-02.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

05-03.text: The United States Senate Committee on Armed Services and United States Senate Committee on Foreign Relations begin their closed door hearings into the dismissal of General Douglas MacArthur by U.S. President Harry Truman.

The police force in Birmingham, Alabama switches tactics and responds with violent force to stop the "Birmingham campaign" protesters. Images of the violent suppression are transmitted worldwide, bringing new-found attention to the Civil Rights Movement.
05-03.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-04.text: The Freedom Rides began, challenging segregation in interstate bus travel.
05-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; interstate commerce; federal enforcement

05-04.text: American civil rights movement: The "Freedom Riders" begin a bus trip through the South.
05-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-05.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

05-06.text: The United States Congress passes the Chinese Exclusion Act.

05-07.text: Michigan ratifies a 203-year-old proposed amendment to the United States Constitution making the 27th Amendment law. This amendment bars the U.S. Congress from giving itself a mid-term pay raise.

05-08.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

05-09.text: Watergate scandal: The United States House Committee on the Judiciary opens formal and public impeachment hearings against President Richard Nixon.

05-10.text: American Revolutionary War: Representatives from the Thirteen Colonies begin the Second Continental Congress in Philadelphia.

05-11.text: An act of the U.S. Congress establishes Glacier National Park in Montana.

05-12.text: Civic focus: Political Socialization — Families, schools, peers, and media shape political beliefs.

05-13.text: Civic focus: Public Opinion — The public’s political attitudes influence elections and policymaking.

05-14.text: In Philadelphia, delegates convene a Constitutional Convention to write a new Constitution for the United States; George Washington presides.
Civil Rights Movement: The Freedom Riders bus is fire-bombed near Anniston, Alabama, and the civil rights protesters are beaten by an angry mob.
05-14.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-15.text: California becomes the second U.S. state after Massachusetts in 2004 to legalize same-sex marriage after the state's own Supreme Court rules a previous ban unconstitutional.
05-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-15.text: In Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey v. United States, the United States Supreme Court declares Standard Oil to be an "unreasonable" monopoly under the Sherman Antitrust Act and orders the company to be broken up.
05-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-16.text: United States President Andrew Johnson is acquitted in his impeachment trial by one vote in the United States Senate.
Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom addresses a joint session of the United States Congress. She is the first British monarch to address the U.S. Congress.
The United States Congress establishes the nickel.

05-17.text: The Supreme Court decided Brown v. Board of Education, ruling school segregation unconstitutional.
05-17.ap_connection: Equal protection; civil rights; Supreme Court

05-17.text: Watergate scandal: Televised hearings begin in the United States Senate.

05-18.text: The Supreme Court decided Plessy v. Ferguson, approving 'separate but equal' segregation.

05-19.text: The United States Congress passes the Emergency Quota Act establishing national quotas on immigration.

Mexican-American War: Mexico ratifies the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo thus ending the war and ceding California, Nevada, Utah and parts of four other modern-day U.S. states to the United States for US$15 million.

05-20.text: Civil rights: The Supreme Court of the United States rules in Romer v. Evans against a law that would have prevented any city, town or county in the state of Colorado from taking any legislative, executive, or judicial action to protect the rights of gays and lesbians.
05-20.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-21.text: American civil rights movement: Alabama Governor John Malcolm Patterson declares martial law in an attempt to restore order after race riots break out.
05-21.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-22.text: Congressman Preston Brooks of South Carolina severely beats Senator Charles Sumner of Massachusetts with a cane in the hall of the United States Senate for a speech Sumner had made regarding Southerners and slavery.
Civil Rights Movement: A jury in Birmingham, Alabama, convicts former Ku Klux Klan member Bobby Frank Cherry of the 1963 murders of four girls in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing.
05-22.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-23.text: South Carolina ratifies the United States Constitution as the eighth American state.

05-24.text: Samuel Morse sends the message "What hath God wrought" (a biblical quotation, Numbers 23:23) from the Old Supreme Court Chamber in the United States Capitol to his assistant, Alfred Vail, in Baltimore, Maryland, to inaugurate a commercial telegraph line between Baltimore and Washington D.C.
05-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-24.text: American civil rights movement: Freedom Riders are arrested in Jackson, Mississippi, for "disturbing the peace" after disembarking from their bus.
05-24.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-24.text: Russia and the United States sign the Moscow Treaty.
05-24.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

05-25.text: The Constitutional Convention opened in Philadelphia.
A treaty between Pennsylvania and Maryland ends the Conojocular War with settlement of a boundary dispute and exchange of prisoners.
05-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-26.text: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that Ellis Island, the historic gateway for millions of immigrants, is mainly in the state of New Jersey, not New York.
05-26.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-26.text: The U.S. Congress passes Public Law 80-557, which permanently establishes the Civil Air Patrol as an auxiliary of the United States Air Force.
The United States and the Soviet Union sign the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty.
05-26.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

05-27.text: New Deal: The Supreme Court of the United States declares the National Industrial Recovery Act to be unconstitutional in A.L.A. Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States, (295 U.S. 495).
05-27.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-28.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

05-29.text: Rhode Island becomes the last of the original United States' colonies to ratify the Constitution and is admitted as the 13th U.S. state.

05-30.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

05-31.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

06-01.text: Louis Brandeis becomes the first Jew appointed to the United States Supreme Court.
06-01.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-02.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

06-03.text: The National Defense Act is signed into law, increasing the size of the United States National Guard by 450,000 men.
06-03.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-04.text: Women's rights: The U.S. Congress approves the 19th Amendment to the United States Constitution, which guarantees suffrage to women, and sends it to the U.S. states for ratification.
06-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-05.text: The U.S. Congress abrogates the United States' use of the gold standard by enacting a joint resolution (48 Stat. 112) nullifying the right of creditors to demand payment in gold.
Louis Brandeis is sworn in as a Justice of the United States Supreme Court; he is the first American Jew to hold such a position.
06-05.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-06.text: In Gonzales v. Raich, the United States Supreme Court upholds a federal law banning cannabis, including medical marijuana.
06-06.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-07.text: The United States Supreme Court overturns the conviction of Paul Cohen for disturbing the peace, setting the precedent that vulgar writing is protected under the First Amendment to the United States Constitution.
06-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-07.text: The Supreme Court of the United States hands down its decision in Griswold v. Connecticut, prohibiting the states from criminalizing the use of contraception by married couples.
06-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-07.text: Richard Henry Lee presents the "Lee Resolution" to the Continental Congress. The motion is seconded by John Adams and will lead to the United States Declaration of Independence.

06-08.text: The United States Supreme Court rules that restaurants in Washington, D.C. cannot refuse to serve black patrons.
06-08.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-09.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

06-10.text: United States Senate breaks a 75-day filibuster against the Civil Rights Act of 1964, leading to the bill's passage.
06-10.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-11.text: Antonio Meucci is acknowledged as the first inventor of the telephone by the United States Congress.
The Continental Congress appoints Thomas Jefferson, John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, Roger Sherman, and Robert R. Livingston to the Committee of Five to draft a declaration of independence.

American Civil Rights Movement: Governor of Alabama George Wallace defiantly stands at the door of Foster Auditorium at the University of Alabama in an attempt to block two black students, Vivian Malone and James Hood, from attending that school. Later in the day, accompanied by federalized National Guard troops, they are able to register.
06-11.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-12.text: The United States Supreme Court in Loving v. Virginia declares all U.S. state laws which prohibit interracial marriage to be unconstitutional.
06-12.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-12.text: NAACP field secretary Medgar Evers is murdered in front of his home in Jackson, Mississippi by Ku Klux Klan member Byron De La Beckwith during the Civil Rights Movement.
06-12.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-13.text: The Supreme Court decided Miranda v. Arizona, requiring warnings before custodial interrogation.
06-13.ap_connection: Rights of the accused; due process; selective incorporation

06-14.text: The Stars and Stripes is adopted by Congress as the Flag of the United States.

06-15.text: The 12th Amendment was ratified, changing the Electoral College process for president and vice president.
New Hampshire approves the Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution, ratifying the document.

The United States Supreme Court rules in United States v. Álvarez-Machaín that it is permissible for the United States to forcibly extradite suspects in foreign countries and bring them to the United States for trial, without approval from those other countries.
06-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-16.text: A treaty annexing the Republic of Hawaii to the United States is signed; the Republic would not be dissolved until a year later.
06-16.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-17.text: The United States Supreme Court rules 8-1 in Abington School District v. Schempp against requiring the reciting of Bible verses and the Lord's Prayer in public schools.
06-17.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-18.text: Civic focus: Agenda Setting — Political institutions and media influence which issues receive public attention.

06-19.text: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 is approved after surviving an 83-day filibuster in the United States Senate.
06-19.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-19.text: The U.S. Congress prohibits slavery in United States territories, nullifying Dred Scott v. Sandford.

06-20.text: The U.S. Congress adopts the Great Seal of the United States.

06-21.text: New Hampshire ratifies the Constitution of the United States and is admitted as the ninth state in the United States.
The U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Texas v. Johnson that American flag-burning was a form of political protest protected by the First Amendment.
06-21.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-21.text: In handing down the decision in Miller v. California 413 US 15, the Supreme Court of the United States establishes the Miller test for obscenity in U.S. law.
06-21.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-22.text: The United States Department of Justice is created by the U.S. Congress.

06-23.text: Title IX of the United States Civil Rights Act of 1964 is amended to prohibit sexual discrimination to any educational program receiving federal funds.
06-23.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-24.text: In Roth v. United States, the U.S. Supreme Court rules that obscenity is not protected by the First Amendment.
06-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-25.text: The Supreme Court decided Engel v. Vitale, ruling school-sponsored prayer unconstitutional.
06-25.ap_connection: Establishment Clause; civil liberties; selective incorporation

06-25.text: Virginia becomes the tenth state to ratify the United States Constitution.
In Clinton v. City of New York, the United States Supreme Court decides that the Line Item Veto Act of 1996 is unconstitutional.
06-25.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-26.text: The Supreme Court decided Obergefell v. Hodges, requiring states to license and recognize same-sex marriages.
06-26.ap_connection: Equal protection; due process; civil rights

06-26.text: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled, 5-4, that same-sex couples have a constitutional right to marriage under the 14th Amendment to the United States Constitution.
06-26.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-26.text: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled, 5-4, that Section 3 of the Defense of Marriage Act is unconstitutional and in violation of the Fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution.
06-26.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-27.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

06-28.text: The United States Supreme Court, in Regents of the University of California v. Bakke bars quota systems in college admissions.
06-28.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-29.text: The United States Supreme Court rules in the case Furman v. Georgia that arbitrary and inconsistent imposition of the death penalty violates the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments, and constitutes cruel and unusual punishment.
06-29.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-29.text: Hyde Park and several other Illinois townships vote to be annexed by Chicago, forming the largest United States city in area and second largest in population at the time.

06-30.text: The United States Congress passes the Meat Inspection Act and Pure Food and Drug Act.
The U.S. Congress organizes the Michigan Territory.

07-01.text: The United States Department of Justice formally comes into existence.

07-02.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
The Continental Congress adopts a resolution severing ties with the Kingdom of Great Britain although the wording of the formal Declaration of Independence is not published until July 4.

07-03.text: The Constitution of Puerto Rico is approved by the United States Congress.
Congress establishes the United States' 2nd mint in San Francisco.

07-04.text: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence.
07-04.ap_connection: Natural rights; popular sovereignty; founding ideals

07-05.text: The National Labor Relations Act, which governs labor relations in the United States, is signed into law by President Franklin D. Roosevelt.
Right to vote: The Twenty-sixth Amendment to the United States Constitution, lowering the voting age from 21 to 18 years, is formally certified by President Richard Nixon.
The Second Continental Congress adopts the Olive Branch Petition.

07-06.text: David Kalākaua, monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaii, is forced to sign the Bayonet Constitution, which transfers much of the king's authority to the Legislature of the Kingdom of Hawaii.

07-07.text: U.S. President Ronald Reagan appoints Sandra Day O'Connor to become the first female member of the Supreme Court of the United States.
07-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-07.text: Buddhist crisis: The police of Ngô Đình Nhu, brother and chief political adviser of President Ngô Đình Diệm, attacked a group of American journalists who were covering a protest.

07-08.text: The Olive Branch Petition is signed by the Continental Congress of the Thirteen Colonies of North America.
Church bells (possibly including the Liberty Bell) are rung after John Nixon delivers the first public reading of the Declaration of Independence of the United States.

07-09.text: The 14th Amendment was ratified, defining citizenship and protecting due process and equal protection.
07-09.ap_connection: Civil rights; due process; equal protection

07-10.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

07-11.text: Former President of the United States William Howard Taft is sworn in as 10th Chief Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, becoming the only person ever to hold both offices.
07-11.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-12.text: The Medal of Honor is authorized by the United States Congress.

07-13.text: The Continental Congress enacts the Northwest Ordinance establishing governing rules for the Northwest Territory. It also establishes procedures for the admission of new states and limits the expansion of slavery.

07-14.text: Civic focus: Linkage Institutions — Parties, elections, interest groups, and media connect people to government.

07-15.text: Civic focus: Voter Turnout — Rules, resources, mobilization, and political interest affect participation.

07-16.text: Congress approved the Residence Act, establishing the future permanent capital on the Potomac River.

07-17.text: Civic focus: Campaign Finance — Rules governing political money shape electoral competition and political speech.

07-18.text: Civic focus: Agenda Setting — Political institutions and media influence which issues receive public attention.

07-19.text: Civic focus: Political Accountability — Elections, oversight, courts, and public scrutiny allow officials to be held responsible.

07-20.text: The United States Department of Justice files suit in New York City against the motion picture industry charging violations of the Sherman Antitrust Act in regards to the studio system. The case would eventually result in a break-up of the industry in 1948.

07-21.text: The United States Senate ratifies the North Atlantic Treaty.

07-22.text: New Deal: The United States Senate votes down President Franklin D. Roosevelt's proposal to add more justices to the Supreme Court of the United States.
07-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-23.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

07-24.text: Watergate scandal: The United States Supreme Court unanimously ruled that President Richard Nixon did not have the authority to withhold subpoenaed White House tapes and they order him to surrender the tapes to the Watergate special prosecutor.
07-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-25.text: American Civil War: The United States Congress passes the Crittenden-Johnson Resolution, stating that the war is being fought to preserve the Union and not to end slavery.

07-26.text: President Harry Truman ordered the desegregation of the U.S. armed forces.
New York ratifies the United States Constitution and becomes the 11th state of the United States.
Hillary Clinton becomes the first female nominee for President of the United States by a major political party at the Democratic National Convention in Philadelphia.

07-27.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

07-28.text: The 14th Amendment to the United States Constitution is certified, establishing African American citizenship and guaranteeing due process of law.

07-29.text: First United States Congress elections in Hawaii as a state of the Union.
United States and Japan sign the Harris Treaty.
07-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-30.text: Watergate scandal: U.S. President Richard Nixon releases subpoenaed White House recordings after being ordered to do so by the Supreme Court of the United States.
07-30.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-31.text: The U.S. Second Continental Congress passes a resolution that the services of Gilbert du Motier "be accepted, and that, in consideration of his zeal, illustrious family and connexions, he have the rank and commission of major-general of the United States."
The United States and Soviet Union both sign the START I Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty, the first to reduce (with verification) both countries' stockpiles.
07-31.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-01.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

08-02.text: The signing of the United States Declaration of Independence took place.

08-03.text: The United States Senate ratifies the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty.
Treaty of Greenville is signed, ending the Northwest Indian War in the Ohio Country.
08-03.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-04.text: U.S. President Jimmy Carter signs legislation creating the United States Department of Energy.
Civil Rights Movement: Civil rights workers Michael Schwerner, Andrew Goodman and James Chaney are found dead in Mississippi after disappearing on June 21.
08-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

08-05.text: The United States, the United Kingdom, and the Soviet Union sign a nuclear test ban treaty.
08-05.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-06.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
Queen Wilhelmina of the Netherlands becomes the first reigning queen to address a joint session of the United States Congress.
Sixty proof sheets of the Constitution of the United States are delivered to the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

08-07.text: Vietnam War: The U.S. Congress passes the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution giving U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson broad war powers to deal with North Vietnamese attacks on American forces.

08-08.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

08-09.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

08-10.text: Japanese American internment: U.S. President Ronald Reagan signs the Civil Liberties Act of 1988, providing $20,000 payments to Japanese Americans who were either interned in or relocated by the United States during World War II.

U.S. President Harry S. Truman signs the National Security Act Amendment, streamlining the defense agencies of the United States government, and replacing the Department of War with the United States Department of Defense.
The Smithsonian Institution is chartered by the United States Congress after James Smithson donates $500,000.

08-11.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

08-12.text: Civic focus: Political Socialization — Families, schools, peers, and media shape political beliefs.

08-13.text: Civic focus: Public Opinion — The public’s political attitudes influence elections and policymaking.

08-14.text: United States Senate leaders agree to rotate the office of President pro tempore of the Senate among leading candidates to fill the vacancy left by William P. Frye's death.
Oregon Territory is organized by act of Congress.

08-15.text: Civic focus: Voter Turnout — Rules, resources, mobilization, and political interest affect participation.

08-16.text: The Migratory Bird Treaty between Canada and the United States signed.
08-16.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-17.text: Civic focus: Campaign Finance — Rules governing political money shape electoral competition and political speech.

08-18.text: The 19th Amendment was ratified, prohibiting voting discrimination based on sex.
The Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, guaranteeing women's suffrage.
08-18.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

08-19.text: War of 1812: American frigate USS Constitution defeats the British frigate HMS Guerriere off the coast of Nova Scotia, Canada earning the nickname "Old Ironsides".

08-20.text: Civic focus: Popular Sovereignty — Government authority comes from the people.

08-21.text: United States President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs an executive order proclaiming Hawaii the 50th state of the union. Hawaii's admission is currently commemorated by Hawaii Admission Day

08-22.text: Alabama Chief Justice Roy Moore is suspended after refusing to comply with a federal court order to remove a rock inscribed with the Ten Commandments from the lobby of the Alabama Supreme Court building.
08-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

08-23.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

08-24.text: Civic focus: Checks and Balances — Each branch has tools to limit the power of the others.

08-25.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

08-26.text: The 19th amendment to United States Constitution takes effect, giving women the right to vote.

08-27.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

08-28.text: The March on Washington brought civil rights activists to the capital and featured Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech.
Black teenager Emmett Till is brutally murdered in Mississippi, galvanizing the nascent Civil Rights Movement.
08-28.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

08-29.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

08-30.text: Thurgood Marshall is confirmed as the first African American Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States.
08-30.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

08-31.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

09-01.text: The United States, Australia and New Zealand sign a mutual defense pact, called the ANZUS Treaty.
09-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-01.text: The Fountain of Time opens as a tribute to the 100 years of peace between the United States and Great Britain following the Treaty of Ghent.
09-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-02.text: Congress created the Department of the Treasury.

09-03.text: American Revolutionary War: The war ends with the signing of the Treaty of Paris by the United States and the Kingdom of Great Britain.

09-04.text: American Civil Rights Movement: Little Rock Crisis: Orval Faubus, governor of Arkansas, calls out the National Guard to prevent African American students from enrolling in Central High School.
09-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

09-05.text: The First Continental Congress convened in Philadelphia.
Russo-Japanese War: In New Hampshire, United States, the Treaty of Portsmouth, mediated by U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt, ends the war.

09-06.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

09-07.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

09-08.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

09-09.text: The Continental Congress officially names its union of states the United States.
The United States Department of Housing and Urban Development is established.

09-10.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

09-11.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

09-12.text: Civic focus: Political Socialization — Families, schools, peers, and media shape political beliefs.

09-13.text: The Philadelphia Convention sets the date for the first presidential election in the United States, and New York City becomes the country's temporary capital.
Margaret Chase Smith is elected United States senator, and becomes the first woman to serve in both the U.S. House of Representatives and the United States Senate.

09-14.text: Civic focus: Linkage Institutions — Parties, elections, interest groups, and media connect people to government.

09-15.text: The Senate Judiciary Committee unanimously approves Sandra Day O'Connor to become the first female justice of the Supreme Court of the United States
09-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

09-15.text: U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson, responding to a sniper attack at the University of Texas at Austin, writes a letter to Congress urging the enactment of gun control legislation.

09-16.text: Civic focus: Interest Groups — Organized groups seek to influence public policy without directly controlling government.

09-17.text: Delegates signed the U.S. Constitution at the Philadelphia Convention.

09-18.text: Margaret Chase Smith of Maine becomes the first woman elected to the United States Senate without completing another senator's term, when she defeats Democratic opponent Adrian Scolten.

09-19.text: The Continental Congress passes the first United States federal budget.

09-20.text: In an address to a joint session of Congress and the American people, U.S. President George W. Bush declares a "War on Terror".

09-21.text: The Defense of Marriage Act passes the United States Congress (a vote of 342-67 in the House of Representatives and a vote of 85-14 in the Senate). The law prohibited federal recognition of same-sex marriage, while allowing states to adopt any marital definition of their choosing.

09-22.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

09-23.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

09-24.text: Civic focus: Checks and Balances — Each branch has tools to limit the power of the others.

09-25.text: Congress proposed the amendments that became the Bill of Rights.
09-25.ap_connection: Civil liberties; amendment process; Anti-Federalist concerns

09-25.text: The United States Congress establishes Sequoia National Park.

09-26.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

09-27.text: The United States Department of Education receives final approval from the U.S. Congress to become the 13th US Cabinet agency.

The Continental Congress appoints John Adams to travel to France as minister plenipotentiary in charge of negotiating treaties of peace and commerce with Great Britain during the American Revolutionary War.

American Revolutionary War: Lancaster, Pennsylvania becomes the capital of the United States, for one day after the Second Continental Congress evacuates Philadelphia to avoid invading British forces.

09-28.text: The Congress of the Confederation votes to send the newly-written United States Constitution to the state legislatures for approval.
American Revolution: Samuel Huntington is elected President of the Continental Congress, succeeding John Jay.

09-30.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

10-01.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

10-02.text: George Washington sends proposed Constitutional amendments (The United States Bill of Rights) to the States for ratification.
Thurgood Marshall is sworn in as the first African-American justice of the United States Supreme Court.
10-02.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

10-03.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

10-04.text: Russian Constitutional Crisis: In Moscow, tanks bombard the White House, a government building that housed the Russian parliament, while demonstrators against President Boris Yeltsin rally outside.

10-05.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

10-06.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

10-07.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

10-08.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

10-09.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

10-10.text: A Mutual Defense Treaty Between the United States and the Republic of Korea is concluded in Washington, D.C.
10-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-11.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

10-12.text: Civic focus: Political Socialization — Families, schools, peers, and media shape political beliefs.

10-13.text: A majority of voters in the Republic of Texas approve a proposed constitution that, if accepted by the U.S. Congress, will make Texas a U.S. state.

10-14.text: Civic focus: Linkage Institutions — Parties, elections, interest groups, and media connect people to government.

10-15.text: The Senate confirmed Clarence Thomas to the Supreme Court after highly contested hearings.
10-15.ap_connection: Judicial appointments; Senate confirmation; public opinion

10-16.text: Civic focus: Interest Groups — Organized groups seek to influence public policy without directly controlling government.

10-17.text: Civic focus: Campaign Finance — Rules governing political money shape electoral competition and political speech.

10-18.text: Civic focus: Agenda Setting — Political institutions and media influence which issues receive public attention.

10-19.text: Civic focus: Political Accountability — Elections, oversight, courts, and public scrutiny allow officials to be held responsible.

10-20.text: The United States Senate ratifies the Louisiana Purchase.

10-21.text: U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs an executive order transferring Wernher von Braun and other German scientists from the United States Army to NASA.

10-22.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

10-23.text: Women's suffrage: In New York City, 25,000-33,000 women march on Fifth Avenue to advocate their right to vote.
10-23.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

10-24.text: Civic focus: Checks and Balances — Each branch has tools to limit the power of the others.

10-25.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

10-26.text: The first Continental Congress adjourns in Philadelphia.

10-27.text: The United States and Spain sign the Treaty of Madrid, which establishes the boundaries between Spanish colonies and the U.S.
10-27.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-28.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

10-29.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

10-30.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

10-31.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

11-01.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

11-02.text: In the United States, KDKA of Pittsburgh starts broadcasting as the first commercial radio station. The first broadcast is the result of the United States presidential election, 1920.
Norman Morrison, a 31-year-old Quaker, sets himself on fire in front of the river entrance to the Pentagon to protest the use of napalm in the Vietnam war.
11-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-03.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt is re-elected President of the United States.
William Howard Taft is elected the 27th President of the United States.
John Willis Menard (R-Louisiana) was the first African American elected to the United States Congress. Because of an electoral challenge, he was never seated.

11-04.text: Barack Obama was elected president, becoming the first Black president of the United States.
Ronald Reagan is elected the 40th President of The United States, defeating incumbent Jimmy Carter.

11-05.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt is the first and only President of the United States to be elected to a third term.
Women's suffrage in the United States: In defiance of the law, suffragist Susan B. Anthony votes for the first time, and is later fined $100.
11-05.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

11-06.text: Barack Obama is reelected President of the United States; Tammy Baldwin becomes the first openly gay politician to be elected to the United States Senate.

11-07.text: Jeannette Rankin became the first woman elected to Congress.
Controversial US presidential election that is later resolved in the Bush v. Gore Supreme Court Case, electing George W. Bush the 43rd President of the United States.
11-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-07.text: The United States Congress overrides President Richard M. Nixon's veto of the War Powers Resolution, which limits presidential power to wage war without congressional approval.

11-08.text: John F. Kennedy was elected president in one of the closest elections in U.S. history.
Donald Trump is elected 45th President of the United States defeating Hillary Clinton. She is the fifth presidential candidate in U.S. history to win the popular vote but lose the election.

Republican Revolution: On the night of the 1994 United States midterm elections, Republicans make historic electoral gains by securing massive majorities in both houses of congress (54 seats in the House and eight seats in the Senate, additionally). Thus bringing a close to four decades of Democratic domination.

11-09.text: Vietnam War: The Supreme Court of the United States votes 6-3 against hearing a case to allow Massachusetts to enforce its law granting residents the right to refuse military service in an undeclared war.
11-09.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-10.text: Civic focus: Due Process — Government must use fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property.

11-11.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.

11-12.text: Civic focus: Political Socialization — Families, schools, peers, and media shape political beliefs.

11-13.text: War on Terror: In the first such act since World War II, US President George W. Bush signs an executive order allowing military tribunals against foreigners suspected of connections to terrorist acts or planned acts on the United States.
The Supreme Court of the United States declares Alabama laws requiring segregated buses illegal, thus ending the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
11-13.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-14.text: Iran hostage crisis: US President Jimmy Carter issues Executive order 12170, freezing all Iranian assets in the United States in response to the hostage crisis.

11-15.text: American Revolutionary War: After 16 months of debate the Continental Congress approves the Articles of Confederation.

11-16.text: The Federal Reserve Bank of the United States officially opens.
11-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-17.text: The United States Congress holds its first session in Washington, D.C.
Articles of Confederation (United States) are submitted to the states for ratification.

11-18.text: Susan B. Anthony and 14 other women are arrested for illegal voting in the United States presidential election of 1872.

The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court rules 4-3 in Goodridge v. Department of Public Health that the state's ban on same-sex marriage is unconstitutional and gives the state legislature 180 days to change the law making Massachusetts the first state in the United States to grant marriage rights to same-sex couples.
11-18.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-19.text: Lewinsky scandal: The United States House of Representatives Judiciary Committee begins impeachment hearings against U.S. President Bill Clinton.
The United States and the Kingdom of Great Britain sign Jay's Treaty, which attempts to resolve some of the lingering problems left over from the American Revolutionary War.

11-20.text: New Jersey becomes the first U.S. state to ratify the Bill of Rights.
Savings and loan crisis: The United States Senate Ethics Committee issues a stern censure of California senator Alan Cranston for his "dealings" with savings-and-loan executive Charles Keating.
The United States Department of Justice files its final anti-trust suit against AT&T Corporation. This suit later leads to the breakup of AT&T and its Bell System.

11-21.text: North Carolina ratifies the United States Constitution and is admitted as the 12th U.S. state.

11-22.text: Civic focus: Federalism — Power is divided between national and state governments.

11-23.text: Civic focus: Separation of Powers — Government power is divided among legislative, executive, and judicial branches.

11-24.text: The Texas Provincial Government authorizes the creation of a horse-mounted police force called the Texas Rangers (which is now the Texas Ranger Division of the Texas Department of Public Safety).
11-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-25.text: The United States Greenback Party is established as a political party consisting primarily of farmers affected by the Panic of 1873.

11-26.text: George W. Bush is certified the winner of Florida's electoral votes by Katherine Harris, going on to win the United States presidential election, despite losing in the national popular vote.
A national Thanksgiving Day is observed in the United States as proclaimed by President George Washington at the request of Congress.

11-27.text: Twenty-fifth Amendment: The United States Senate votes 92-3 to confirm Gerald Ford as Vice President of the United States. (On December 6, the House will confirm him 387-35).

11-28.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

11-29.text: Civic focus: Selective Incorporation — Most Bill of Rights protections apply to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

11-30.text: The Democratic-Republican-controlled United States Senate begins an impeachment trial of Federalist Supreme Court Justice Samuel Chase.
11-30.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

12-01.text: Rosa Parks was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama, helping spark the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
12-01.ap_connection: Civil rights; social movements; equal protection

12-01.text: United States presidential election, 1824: Since no candidate received a majority of the total electoral college votes in the election, the United States House of Representatives is given the task of deciding the winner in accordance with the Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution.

12-02.text: Vietnam War: After a trip to Vietnam at the request of U.S. President John F. Kennedy, U.S. Senate Majority Leader Mike Mansfield becomes the first American official to comment adversely on the war's progress.

12-03.text: In Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, representatives from 121 countries sign the Ottawa Treaty prohibiting manufacture and deployment of anti-personnel landmines. The United States, People's Republic of China, and Russia do not sign the treaty, however.
12-03.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-04.text: By a vote of 65-7, the United States Senate approves United States participation in the United Nations. (The UN had been established on October 24, 1945.)

12-05.text: The 21st Amendment was ratified, repealing Prohibition.
The Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified.
California Gold Rush: In a message to the United States Congress, U.S. President James K. Polk confirms that large amounts of gold had been discovered in California.

12-06.text: The Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, banning slavery.

The Twenty-fifth Amendment: The United States House of Representatives votes 387 to 35 to confirm Gerald Ford as Vice President of the United States. (On November 27, the Senate confirmed him 92 to 3.)
The U.S. Congress moves from New York City to Philadelphia.

12-07.text: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, leading Congress to declare war the next day.
Delaware becomes the first state to ratify the United States Constitution.

12-08.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

12-09.text: American Civil War: The Joint Committee on the Conduct of the War is established by the U.S. Congress.
The Governor of Illinois, Rod Blagojevich, is arrested by federal officials for crimes including attempting to sell the U.S. Senate seat being vacated by President-elect Barack Obama.

12-10.text: Wyoming Territory granted women the right to vote, decades before the 19th Amendment.

12-11.text: The U.S. Senate creates a select committee on finance and a uniform national currency, predecessor of the United States Senate Committee on Finance.

12-12.text: Pennsylvania becomes the second state to ratify the United States Constitution, five days after Delaware became the first.
The United States Supreme Court releases its decision in Bush v. Gore.
12-12.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

12-13.text: Civic focus: Public Opinion — The public’s political attitudes influence elections and policymaking.

12-14.text: American Civil Rights Movement: Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that Congress can use the Constitution's Commerce Clause to fight discrimination.
12-14.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

12-15.text: The Bill of Rights was ratified, protecting key civil liberties against the national government.
12-15.ap_connection: Bill of Rights; civil liberties; amendments

12-15.text: The Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution officially becomes effective, repealing the Eighteenth Amendment that prohibited the sale, manufacture, and transportation of alcohol.

12-16.text: Civic focus: Interest Groups — Organized groups seek to influence public policy without directly controlling government.

12-17.text: The American Civil Rights Congress delivers "We Charge Genocide" to the United Nations.
12-17.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

12-18.text: The 13th Amendment was ratified, abolishing slavery in the United States.
12-18.ap_connection: Civil rights; Reconstruction amendments; federal power

12-18.text: New Jersey becomes the third state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.

12-19.text: The House of Representatives impeached President Bill Clinton.
Nelson Rockefeller is sworn in as Vice President of the United States under President Gerald Ford under the provisions of the 25th Amendment to the United States Constitution.
Nullification Crisis: Vice President of the United States John C. Calhoun pens the South Carolina Exposition and Protest, protesting the Tariff of 1828.

12-20.text: Civic focus: Popular Sovereignty — Government authority comes from the people.

12-21.text: Civic focus: Rule of Law — Public officials and citizens are bound by the law.

12-22.text: The repeal of the Don't ask, don't tell policy, the 17-year-old policy banning homosexuals serving openly in the United States military, is signed into law by President Barack Obama.

12-23.text: The Federal Reserve Act is signed into law by President Woodrow Wilson, creating the Federal Reserve System.

12-24.text: Representatives of Britain and the United States sign the Treaty of Ghent, ending the War of 1812.
12-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-25.text: Civic focus: Judicial Review — Courts may invalidate government actions that conflict with the Constitution.

12-26.text: Civic focus: Congressional Oversight — Congress monitors executive agencies and implementation of federal law.

12-27.text: Civic focus: Executive Orders — Presidents direct executive-branch operations without passing a new statute.

12-28.text: Civic focus: Bureaucratic Discretion — Agencies make choices about how laws and regulations are implemented.

12-29.text: The Treaty of New Echota is signed, ceding all the lands of the Cherokee east of the Mississippi River to the United States.
12-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-30.text: The Treaty of St. Louis between the United States and the Shawnee Nation is proclaimed.
12-30.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-30.text: The Treaty of St. Louis (1816) between the United States and the united Ottawa, Ojibwa, and Potawatomi Indian tribes is proclaimed.
12-30.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-31.text: Civic focus: Equal Protection — States must provide equal protection of the laws.
